rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is a probable acute surgical abdomen (appendicitis) with localised peritonism. It needs urgent, same-day Emergency Department assessment for bloods, imaging and surgical review — the baseline is T4 (ED, within hours, self/escorted transport). It is not, at baseline, an immediate minutes-to-live emergency requiring an ambulance, so T5 is reserved for perforation/peritonitis or shock. Under-triage (managing at home, urgent-care-without-surgical-access, or routine GP) is the dangerous error here; over-triage to 000 is safe.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Persistent localised right-lower-abdominal pain with anorexia and movement-sensitive pain
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend the Emergency Department today, within hours
escalation_edges.rationale: A probable surgical abdomen requires same-day bloods, imaging and surgical review; it cannot be safely worked up or excluded remotely.
escalation_edges.communication_template: The pattern of your pain needs to be checked in hospital today. Please go to the Emergency Department now for blood tests, a scan and a surgical review — this isn't something we can sort out safely over the phone.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden relief of the pain followed by high fever and worsening, spreading generalised illness (perforation/peritonitis)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately if this happens at any time
escalation_edges.rationale: Sudden pain relief can mean the appendix has perforated; progression to generalised peritonitis and sepsis is life-threatening and needs an emergency ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Important: if the pain suddenly disappears and then you get a high fever, spreading tummy pain or feel very unwell, do not wait — call 000 for an ambulance straight away. That can be a sign the appendix has burst.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fainting, a racing heart, spreading rigidity of the abdomen or feeling severely unwell before reaching hospital
escalation_edges.rationale: These suggest peritonitis with early sepsis or shock — an immediate ambulance is needed rather than self-transport.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel faint, your heart is racing, your whole tummy goes hard, or you feel severely unwell before you get to hospital, call 000 for an ambulance.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and relies on public transport, which she feels unable to manage in her current state
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not rely on the patient travelling independently: arrange a relative/taxi escort to the ED, and lower the threshold to call 000 for ambulance transport if no safe escort is available.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one at home to monitor her or act if she deteriorates
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Because she cannot be observed at home, home-based watchful waiting is not appropriate; ensure she is en route to hospital and knows the 000 triggers.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Go to the Emergency Department today for assessment — you need blood tests, a scan and a surgical review to find out what is causing this pain. Stay nil by mouth (nothing to eat or drink) in case you need an operation. Arrange for someone to take you or take a taxi — do not drive yourself.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend the Emergency Department today; call your emergency contact for an escort. Call 000 if any danger signs appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: The Emergency Department is open 24 hours — do not wait for GP hours; this needs assessment today.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Advise the patient to bring any hospital discharge summary to a GP review 3–5 days after discharge for wound and recovery checks.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a probable acute surgical abdomen as constipation, gastroenteritis or a minor problem manageable at home or at a routine GP visit.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delayed diagnosis allows the appendix to perforate, causing generalised peritonitis, sepsis and possible death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing to same-day urgent care without ensuring rapid access to bloods, imaging and surgical referral, delaying surgical assessment while inflammation progresses toward perforation.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling 000 for a haemodynamically stable patient who could be safely escorted to the ED — ambulance resources used unnecessarily; safe for the patient but lower-value, and acceptable here given the diagnosis and social isolation.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Appendicitis cannot be confirmed or excluded via telehealth, and gynaecological mimics (ectopic, ovarian torsion) remain in play in a reproductive-age female.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Adopt a high-suspicion, escalate-and-workup stance: send for same-day ED bloods and imaging, keep nil by mouth, and safety-net for perforation — rather than attempting a remote rule-in or rule-out.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the uncertainty to justify watchful waiting at home, a routine GP appointment, or a remote 'probably just a bug' reassurance.
